steps.content: Programs are much more interesting when they can respond to the user. Python's input() function lets you ask the user for information and store their response.
The input() function displays a prompt message and waits for the user to type something. Whatever the user types is returned as a string.

name = input("Enter your name: ")
print("Hello, " + name + "!")
input() always returns a string — even if the user types a number! If you type 42, you get the string "42", not the integer 42. You must convert it if you need to do math.
Use int() to convert a string to an integer, or float() to convert to a decimal number. This is called type conversion or casting.

age = input("How old are you? ")
age = int(age)
next_year = age + 1
print(f"Next year you'll be {next_year}")
Combine input() with f-strings to create friendly, personalized messages. This is one of the most common patterns in beginner programs.

city = input("What city do you live in? ")
print(f"{city} sounds like a great place!")
You can even call int() and input() together on one line. This asks for input and immediately converts it to a number.

name = input("What's your name? ")
age = int(input("How old are you? "))
print(f"Hi {name}! In 10 years, you'll be {age + 10}.")
Always convert user input as early as possible — right after you get it. This makes your code cleaner and catches conversion errors in one predictable place.
